title: GraphQL Interview
description: Senior-level GraphQL interview quiz covering schema design, Apollo Server, Node.js integration, performance, security, and evolution

questions.question: What is the strongest reason teams adopt GraphQL over many REST endpoints?
options: It eliminates the need for server-side authorization
It lets clients request exactly the data they need through a typed contract
It automatically makes every request faster than REST
It removes the need for schema design
questions.explanation: GraphQL is most valuable when clients have diverse data needs. The explicit schema and field selection reduce over-fetching and under-fetching while preserving a typed contract.

questions.question: Why is a GraphQL schema more than just documentation?
options: It serves as an executable contract for validation, tooling, and client queries
It only matters for code generation
It replaces runtime resolvers entirely
It is only useful in development
questions.explanation: The schema defines the API surface that clients can query and tools can inspect. It drives validation, autocomplete, introspection, and type generation.

questions.question: What is the main architectural risk of exposing too many nested fields in a GraphQL schema?
options: Clients can no longer use fragments
Queries can become expensive and hard to control without complexity limits and good resolver design
Subscriptions stop working
The schema becomes impossible to introspect
questions.explanation: GraphQL’s flexibility can create expensive query shapes. Senior teams protect the server with depth, complexity, and performance controls.

questions.question: What is the N+1 problem in GraphQL?
options: A single query returning one extra field
A pattern where resolving nested fields triggers many repeated downstream fetches
A subscription that never reconnects
A schema validation error caused by duplicate types
questions.explanation: N+1 happens when a parent list causes one query, and each child field resolution triggers additional data loads. This can be severe without batching or joins.

questions.question: Why is DataLoader commonly used in Apollo GraphQL servers?
options: To batch and cache data fetches during a request, reducing duplicate backend calls
To replace the GraphQL schema
To encrypt response payloads
To generate client queries automatically
questions.explanation: DataLoader is a request-scoped batching and caching utility. It helps collapse repeated resolver lookups into fewer backend calls.

questions.question: What is the safest place to instantiate DataLoader in a Node GraphQL app?
options: Once globally for the entire process
Per request, usually in the context object
Only inside the database layer
Only in client code
questions.explanation: DataLoader should be request-scoped so cache entries do not leak between users or requests and batching stays isolated to a single operation.

questions.question: Why are GraphQL resolvers ideally thin?
options: They should focus on orchestration while domain logic stays in services or data layers
They must contain all validation and persistence code
They cannot call other internal services
They should always return cached data only
questions.explanation: Thin resolvers are easier to test and evolve. Senior architectures keep business logic in dedicated layers rather than embedding it in field resolvers.

questions.question: What is a key advantage of GraphQL fragments?
options: They allow reuse of field selections and improve consistency across queries
They eliminate the need for variables
They make introspection impossible
They replace mutation inputs
questions.explanation: Fragments help clients reuse common field groups and keep query documents maintainable as schema usage grows.

questions.question: What is the role of variables in a GraphQL operation?
options: They hardcode values into the query document
They separate dynamic input from the query text and improve safety and cacheability
They are only used in subscriptions
They replace GraphQL types
questions.explanation: Variables keep the operation reusable and reduce the need to interpolate values into query text, improving hygiene and operational safety.

questions.question: What is a practical benefit of persisted queries in production GraphQL systems?
options: They let the client send a query hash instead of the full document, reducing payload size and limiting arbitrary query execution
They remove the need for authorization
They disable schema validation
They guarantee no N+1 problems
questions.explanation: Persisted queries reduce transport overhead and allow servers to control the set of accepted operations, which helps performance and security.

questions.question: Why do production GraphQL servers often enforce query depth or complexity limits?
options: To prevent expensive or abusive queries from overloading resolvers and backing services
To make fragments illegal
To stop introspection on the client
To force every request to use GET
questions.explanation: GraphQL’s expressive query language can be abused accidentally or intentionally. Limits help protect availability and latency.

questions.question: What is the most accurate statement about GraphQL caching?
options: GraphQL cannot be cached at all
Caching is possible, but the strategy often shifts from endpoint-level caching to field, entity, persisted-query, or CDN-aware approaches
Only mutations can be cached
Apollo Server disables all HTTP caching by design
questions.explanation: GraphQL responses are query-shape dependent, so caching needs more nuance than traditional REST endpoint caching. Entity and persisted-query strategies are common.

questions.question: Why does GraphQL make client-side caching more complicated than REST in many apps?
options: Responses are normalized by object identity and query shape, not only by URL
GraphQL responses cannot contain nested data
Apollo Client only caches mutations
GraphQL has no notion of IDs
questions.explanation: Clients usually normalize by typename plus id and merge results from many queries. That flexibility is powerful, but it also requires good cache identity design.

questions.question: What is the best reason to include stable IDs on object types in GraphQL?
options: To support normalization, refetching, and entity consistency across queries
To make all fields required
To prevent schema introspection
To avoid using fragments
questions.explanation: Stable IDs are foundational for normalized caching and client-side entity reuse, especially in Apollo Client.

questions.question: What is a senior-level concern when designing GraphQL mutations?
options: Mutations should model clear side effects, validation, and idempotency where appropriate
Mutations should always return only a boolean
Mutations should never include input types
Mutations are only for admin users
questions.explanation: Well-designed mutations express intent clearly, validate inputs, and consider retry behavior and consistency guarantees.

questions.question: Why are input types recommended for GraphQL mutations?
options: They group related fields, improve validation, and make APIs easier to evolve
They eliminate resolver code
They are required for subscriptions
They only exist for code generation
questions.explanation: Input objects create clearer mutation contracts and keep inputs evolvable as requirements change.

questions.question: What does GraphQL nullability communicate to clients?
options: Only runtime defaults
Whether a field may legitimately be absent or unresolved, which affects client error handling and UI logic
That the field will always be returned with a default value
That the schema is optional
questions.explanation: Nullability is a contract. It tells clients what they must guard for and how failures can propagate through nested selections.

questions.question: Why is overly aggressive non-null usage risky in GraphQL schemas?
options: It can cause failure propagation that nulls out larger parts of the response
It makes the schema impossible to validate
It disables fragments
It prevents batching
questions.explanation: Non-null fields are strict. If one cannot be resolved, GraphQL may propagate the error upward and affect more of the payload than expected.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of schema stitching or federation in larger organizations?
options: It allows teams to compose subgraphs or schemas while preserving service ownership boundaries
It removes the need for any gateway
It forces all data into one database
It eliminates the need for versioning
questions.explanation: Composition lets teams own separate domains while presenting a unified graph to clients. Governance and cross-service contracts still matter a lot.

questions.question: What is the role of Apollo Router or a GraphQL gateway in federated setups?
options: To compose and route queries across subgraphs while enforcing cross-cutting policies
To replace all backend services
To execute database joins directly
To store user sessions
questions.explanation: A gateway or router orchestrates query planning and policy enforcement across composed schemas, but domain logic remains in subgraphs or services.

questions.question: Why is GraphQL federation operationally harder than a single monolith schema?
options: It introduces cross-team schema ownership, composition validation, and routing complexity
It cannot use TypeScript
It disables query validation
It only works over WebSocket
questions.explanation: Distributed ownership increases coordination needs. Composition errors, version drift, and query planning complexity are the tradeoffs for autonomy.

questions.question: What is a good reason to keep GraphQL resolvers side-effect free when possible?
options: Pure resolvers are easier to cache, test, and reason about
GraphQL forbids side effects in all cases
It makes subscriptions impossible
It removes the need for authentication
questions.explanation: Query resolvers should ideally be deterministic. Side effects belong in mutations or explicit workflows, not in data fetching.

questions.question: How should errors be modeled in GraphQL for mature clients?
options: Use a consistent error contract and understand partial data plus errors can coexist in one response
Throw away all field errors and always return HTTP 500
Hide errors inside random string fields
Never return any errors to clients
questions.explanation: GraphQL can return data and errors together. Senior designs document how business failures, auth failures, and partial resolution should behave.

questions.question: What is the value of GraphQL unions and interfaces?
options: They model polymorphic result sets with explicit runtime type resolution
They replace input types
They are only for client code generation
They prevent fragment usage
questions.explanation: Unions and interfaces let a schema represent variants in a typed way, which is useful for search results, feeds, and heterogeneous domain objects.

questions.question: Why are __typename fields useful in Apollo Client workflows?
options: They help normalize cached entities and discriminate between union/interface members
They are only decorative and can be ignored
They replace IDs
They disable query planning
questions.explanation: __typename gives the client a stable type discriminator, which is essential for cache normalization and fragment resolution.

questions.question: What is the senior-level stance on GraphQL subscriptions?
options: Use them for real-time needs with careful attention to transport, scaling, and auth semantics
Use them as a general replacement for queries
They always work identically over HTTP GET
They eliminate the need for event streams
questions.explanation: Subscriptions are valuable for push-based updates, but transport choice, fan-out, reconnection, and authorization are major design concerns.

questions.question: What is a practical challenge of scaling GraphQL subscriptions horizontally?
options: Keeping connected clients, event fan-out, and stateful session handling consistent across nodes
Making queries valid
Generating schemas
Parsing variables
questions.explanation: Subscription servers often need shared pub/sub infrastructure and sticky-session or external state strategies to scale reliably.

questions.question: What is a good production reason to use GraphQL over HTTP POST rather than GET for many queries?
options: POST avoids URL length issues and is often simpler for complex variable payloads
GET cannot be cached by any CDN
POST is required by the GraphQL spec for all operations
POST automatically batches multiple operations
questions.explanation: Complex query documents and variables often exceed practical URL limits. POST is the common choice, though GET can still be useful for persisted queries.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of introspection in GraphQL?
options: It enables schema discovery for tooling, codegen, and client development
It encrypts queries in transit
It replaces authorization rules
It is only used by Apollo Studio
questions.explanation: Introspection is foundational to developer tooling and schema exploration, though some production systems limit it selectively for security or governance reasons.

questions.question: Why might production systems disable or restrict introspection in certain environments?
options: To reduce schema exposure and limit information available to unauthenticated clients
Because GraphQL cannot function otherwise
To improve resolver performance by 50 percent automatically
To make queries smaller on the wire
questions.explanation: Restricting introspection can be a security and governance measure, especially for public or sensitive APIs, but it must be balanced against developer experience.

questions.question: What is a key benefit of schema-first development with Apollo?
options: It aligns backend and frontend teams around an explicit contract before implementation details solidify
It removes the need for server code
It guarantees performance improvements
It eliminates schema governance
questions.explanation: Schema-first helps teams design the contract, generate types, and iterate on the API surface with less ambiguity.

questions.question: Why should GraphQL server logging include operation names?
options: They improve tracing, incident triage, and query usage analysis
They replace request IDs
They are required for subscriptions only
They are used to compute schema hashes
questions.explanation: Operation names make it easier to understand client behavior, detect hotspots, and correlate traffic patterns with performance issues.

questions.question: What should a senior engineer watch for when enabling caching in Apollo Server?
options: Different query shapes, user-specific data, and authorization-sensitive fields that should not be shared blindly
Only the number of resolver files
Whether the schema uses enums
Whether introspection is enabled
questions.explanation: Caching must respect identity and authorization boundaries. Shared caches can leak data if user context and cache keys are not designed carefully.

questions.question: What is the most accurate statement about GraphQL and authorization?
options: Authorization should be enforced at the field and business-rule level, not only at the transport layer
The schema itself provides authorization automatically
Only mutations need auth
Authentication and authorization are the same thing
questions.explanation: GraphQL’s flexibility means sensitive fields can appear anywhere. Auth checks should be applied in resolvers or domain services where context is available.

questions.question: Why is field-level authorization often preferable to only top-level operation checks?
options: Because access control can vary by object and field even within the same query
Because it is always faster
Because top-level checks are invalid in GraphQL
Because clients cannot request nested fields otherwise
questions.explanation: Different users may be allowed to see different slices of the same object. Fine-grained authorization matches the graph model better.

questions.question: What is one reason GraphQL can be a poor fit for highly cacheable, static content delivery?
options: The response shape is client-defined and may vary widely, making generic caching less straightforward
GraphQL cannot serve static pages
Apollo Server cannot run on Node
GraphQL responses are always uncacheable
questions.explanation: Client-defined shape is powerful, but it reduces the simplicity of standard HTTP caching compared with fixed REST resources.

questions.question: What is a good use case for GraphQL in a Node backend with Apollo?
options: Aggregating multiple backend systems into one client-facing contract with tailored field selection
Replacing all databases
Serving raw TCP streams
Avoiding any service composition
questions.explanation: GraphQL is strongest when it acts as an aggregation and composition layer over multiple data sources and services.

questions.question: What is the strongest reason to keep GraphQL business logic out of Apollo-specific glue as much as possible?
options: It makes the domain easier to test, reuse, and migrate if transport changes later
Apollo resolvers cannot call services
It reduces schema size automatically
It disables code generation
questions.explanation: Transport-specific code should stay thin. Keeping business logic in shared services or domain modules preserves architecture flexibility.

questions.question: Why are GraphQL batch requests not a complete substitute for resolver batching?
options: They group operations at the HTTP layer but do not automatically solve repeated backend lookups inside a single operation
They only work with mutations
They eliminate the need for query planning
They require WebSocket transport
questions.explanation: HTTP-level batching can reduce request overhead, but N+1 problems still happen inside an individual operation unless resolver-level batching is also used.

questions.question: What distinguishes a senior GraphQL engineer from a junior one most clearly?
options: They balance schema design, performance, caching, authorization, and operational governance instead of treating GraphQL as only a query syntax
They can write the longest query document
They avoid Apollo tools entirely
They assume GraphQL removes the need for backend architecture
questions.explanation: Senior GraphQL work is about operating a contract-driven distributed system responsibly, including performance, security, and evolution concerns.
